one_liner: A 48-year-old male with type 2 diabetes and joint pain presents with fatigue, elevated liver enzymes, and a bronze skin discoloration for 6 months.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient presents with progressive fatigue and malaise over the past 6 months. He was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes mellitus 1 year ago at age 47, which his PCP noted was unusual given his lean body habitus. He has developed joint pain and stiffness in his second and third MCP joints bilaterally, with a firm handshake becoming painful. His wife has noticed his skin has taken on a darker, bronze-gray hue that is not related to sun exposure. He reports decreased libido and erectile dysfunction for 6 months. His PCP found persistently elevated liver enzymes on routine labs. He denies alcohol use, hepatitis risk factors, or use of hepatotoxic medications. He initially attributed his symptoms to stress and aging.
stage2.pmh: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (diagnosed 1 year ago, on metformin with moderate control), erectile dysfunction
stage2.medications: Metformin 1000 mg twice daily, sildenafil 50 mg as needed
stage2.family_history: Father died of liver cirrhosis at age 62 (attributed to alcohol though he was reportedly a light drinker). Brother has type 2 diabetes. Northern European (Irish-Scottish) ancestry.
stage2.social_history: No alcohol use. Non-smoker. Works as an engineer. Married. No tattoos. No IV drug use. No blood transfusion history.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Lean male with generalized bronze-gray skin discoloration. HEENT: No scleral icterus. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: Hepatomegaly with liver palpable 4 cm below the right costal margin, smooth and non-tender. No splenomegaly. No ascites. MSK: Firm, bony enlargement of second and third MCP joints bilaterally with mild tenderness and decreased range of motion. No synovitis. Extremities: No edema. Skin: Diffuse bronze-gray hyperpigmentation most prominent on sun-exposed areas and the axillae. Hair loss in axillary and pubic regions. Testes: Bilateral testicular atrophy noted on exam.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated ferritin strongly suggests iron overload; ferritin >1000 raises concern for organ damage
Transferrin saturation >45% is the best initial screening test for hemochromatosis; >60% is highly suggestive
Markedly elevated serum iron consistent with iron overload
Normal TIBC with high iron results in markedly elevated transferrin saturation
Elevated from hepatic iron deposition and liver injury
Elevated transaminases with ALT > AST pattern suggests non-cirrhotic liver injury from iron deposition
Diabetes from iron deposition in pancreatic beta cells — a classic feature of hemochromatosis (bronze diabetes)
Hypogonadism from iron deposition in the pituitary (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism)
Low LH confirms hypogonadotropic (central) hypogonadism from pituitary iron deposition
Low FSH further supports central hypogonadism
C282Y homozygosity is the most common genotype in hereditary hemochromatosis; confirms the diagnosis
Rules out viral hepatitis as a cause of elevated liver enzymes

imaging_results.result: Diffusely decreased signal intensity of the liver on T2*-weighted sequences consistent with hepatic iron overload. Liver iron concentration estimated at 280 μmol/g dry weight (markedly elevated). No focal liver lesions. No evidence of cirrhosis.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: MRI is the non-invasive gold standard for quantifying hepatic iron content; confirms significant iron overload

imaging_results.result: Hook-like osteophytes at the second and third MCP joints bilaterally. Joint space narrowing with subchondral sclerosis. Chondrocalcinosis of the triangular fibrocartilage of the wrist.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Hemochromatosis arthropathy classically involves 2nd and 3rd MCP joints with hook-like osteophytes; chondrocalcinosis from associated CPPD disease

low_yield_tests: Liver biopsy (not needed if genotype confirmed and ferritin < 1000 without liver disease signs)
Serum ceruloplasmin (Wilson disease unlikely at this age)
Abdominal CT (MRI is superior for iron quantification)

stage5.final_clue: HFE gene testing confirms C282Y homozygosity, and family screening reveals that the patient's brother is also C282Y homozygous with elevated ferritin — his father's 'alcoholic' cirrhosis was likely undiagnosed hemochromatosis.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hereditary hemochromatosis classically presents in middle-aged men (women present later due to menses) of Northern European ancestry with the triad of cirrhosis, diabetes mellitus, and skin hyperpigmentation (bronze diabetes). Additional features include arthropathy (especially 2nd/3rd MCP joints), hypogonadism, and cardiomyopathy. However, the classic full triad is late-stage disease — most patients are now detected earlier through abnormal iron studies or family screening.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: This patient was initially misdiagnosed with typical type 2 diabetes and attributed his joint symptoms to osteoarthritis. The atypical features that should raise suspicion include: diabetes in a lean individual, arthropathy affecting MCP joints rather than DIPs (which would be typical OA), bronze skin discoloration, erectile dysfunction at a young age, and a family history of unexplained liver disease. The diagnosis was delayed because each symptom was attributed to a common condition rather than recognized as part of a unifying diagnosis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Hereditary hemochromatosis is an autosomal recessive disorder most commonly caused by the C282Y mutation in the HFE gene. The HFE protein normally interacts with transferrin receptors to regulate hepcidin production. Hepcidin is the master iron regulatory hormone that controls intestinal iron absorption and macrophage iron release. In hemochromatosis, hepcidin levels are inappropriately low, leading to unregulated intestinal iron absorption (2-3x normal). Over decades, excess iron deposits in parenchymal cells of the liver (causing cirrhosis), pancreas (diabetes), heart (cardiomyopathy), pituitary (hypogonadism), joints (arthropathy), and skin (hyperpigmentation from melanin stimulation).
debrief.treatment_overview: Phlebotomy is the cornerstone of treatment: initially remove 500 mL of blood (equivalent to 250 mg iron) weekly until ferritin reaches 50-100 ng/mL and transferrin saturation normalizes. Maintenance phlebotomy every 2-4 months to keep ferritin < 50-100 ng/mL. Monitor ferritin and CBC before each session. In patients who cannot tolerate phlebotomy, iron chelation therapy (deferoxamine or deferasirox) is an alternative. Organ-specific management: optimize diabetes control, testosterone replacement for symptomatic hypogonadism, cardiac monitoring. Screen all first-degree relatives with iron studies and HFE genotyping. Hepatocellular carcinoma screening with ultrasound and AFP every 6 months if cirrhosis is present.
high_yield_pearls: Transferrin saturation >45% is the best initial screening test; serum ferritin reflects total body iron burden and degree of organ damage
C282Y homozygosity accounts for 85-90% of hereditary hemochromatosis cases in individuals of Northern European descent
Arthropathy is the most common symptom that brings patients to medical attention and often does NOT improve with phlebotomy
Hemochromatosis is associated with an increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma — screening is essential in patients with cirrhosis
sources: UpToDate: Clinical manifestations and diagnosis of hereditary hemochromatosis

after_stage2_more_likely: Hemochromatosis (lean male with diabetes, bronze skin, arthropathy of 2nd/3rd MCPs, hepatomegaly, hypogonadism, Northern European ancestry, family history of liver disease)
after_labs_top3: Alcoholic liver disease with iron deposition
